text.title: Health Checks, Disaster Recovery, and Chaos Engineering
text.summary: Kubernetes probe design, DR strategy selection by RTO/RPO, 3-2-1 backup rule, and the chaos engineering maturity model for validating reliability assumptions.

section.title: Kubernetes Health Probes

rule.statement: Liveness probe must check only the process itself (no external deps); readiness probe must check all critical dependencies.
rule.rationale: If liveness checks the database and the database is slow, Kubernetes kills and restarts the pod — which does not fix the database. Liveness should only detect deadlocks and infinite loops. Readiness correctly removes the pod from load balancer rotation when deps are unavailable.

rule.statement: Use a startup probe for slow-starting applications with failureThreshold * periodSeconds greater than the maximum startup time.
rule.rationale: Without startup probe, liveness begins immediately and kills the pod before it finishes initialising — causing a restart loop.

list.item: GET /health/live → liveness: 200 OK if process running, no external checks
GET /health/ready → readiness: checks DB, cache, critical external APIs; returns 503 if any critical dependency unhealthy
GET /health/startup → startup: verifies essential initialisation complete
GET /health/detailed → internal only (not exposed publicly); full dependency status with latencies

section.reference: FastAPI liveness/readiness/startup endpoints with concurrent dependency checks

rule.statement: Select a DR strategy based on explicit RTO and RPO requirements, not by default to the cheapest option.
rule.rationale: Backup/Restore is cheap but has hours of RTO — unacceptable for a payment API with a 15-minute SLA. Matching strategy to requirement avoids both over-spending and under-protecting.

list.item: Backup/Restore: RTO hours, RPO hours, cost $. Use for non-critical workloads.
Pilot Light: RTO 10-30 min, RPO minutes, cost $$. Core services warm, scale on failure.
Warm Standby: RTO minutes, RPO seconds, cost $$$. Scaled-down copy always running.
Hot Standby / Multi-Active: RTO seconds/zero, RPO near-zero, cost $$$$. Mission-critical only.

rule.statement: Apply the 3-2-1 backup rule: 3 copies, 2 different storage media types, 1 offsite location.
rule.rationale: A single backup in the same region as production is destroyed by the same event (region outage, ransomware) that caused the original failure.

section.title: Chaos Engineering

rule.statement: Define steady-state hypothesis before each chaos experiment; abort if SLO is breached during the experiment.
rule.rationale: Without a hypothesis, chaos is destructive testing not learning. Aborting on SLO breach limits blast radius and preserves the error budget.

section.p: Maturity levels: 1=Manual failover game days, 2=Automated chaos in staging, 3=Production chaos at low traffic, 4=Continuous always-on chaos. Progress sequentially — never start at level 3 without level 2 confidence.

list.item: Instance termination: tests auto-scaling and failover
Network latency injection (200ms): tests timeout and retry behaviour
CPU/memory stress: tests autoscaling and throttling
